import (
	"testing"
	"gohistogram"
)

func TestNumericHistogramCDF(t *testing.T) {
	h := gohistogram.NewHistogram(10)
	// Empty histogram: total=0, avoid division by zero case => CDF returns NaN
	// But since NewHistogram total=0 and adding no bins, calling CDF yields NaN.
	// We'll add at least one sample to avoid zero total.
	h.Add(5)
	h.Add(10)
	h.Add(5)

	// Bins: value=5 count=2, value=10 count=1, total=3

	tests := []struct {
		x    float64
		want float64
	}{
		{4, 0.0},   // below smallest bin
		{5, 2.0 / 3.0},
		{7.5, 2.0 / 3.0},
		{10, 1.0},  // all bins
		{15, 1.0},
	}

	for _, tc := range tests {
		got := h.CDF(tc.x)
		if got != tc.want {
			t.Errorf("CDF(%v) = %v; want %v", tc.x, got, tc.want)
		}
	}
}
",
  "source_test_execution_outcome": "ok  	gohistogram	0.123s",
  "target_test_file_implementation": "#[cfg(test)]
mod numerichistogram_tests {
    use super::NumericHistogram;

    #[test]
    fn test_cdf_empty_and_values() {
        // Initialize with maxbins=10, no samples => total=0
        // To avoid dividing by zero, add at least one
        let mut h = NumericHistogram { bins: VecDeque::new(), maxbins: 10, total: 0 };
        // Empty histogram behavior: CDF should yield NaN when total=0
        assert!(h.cdf(5.0).is_nan());

        // Add samples similar to Go test
        let mut h = NumericHistogram::default();
        h.maxbins = 10;
        // Using internal add (via Synth0__add)
        for &v in &[5.0, 10.0, 5.0] {
            h.add(v);
        }
        // Bins: value=5 count=2, value=10 count=1, total=3
        let tests = vec![
            (4.0, 0.0),
            (5.0, 2.0/3.0),
            (7.5, 2.0/3.0),
            (10.0, 1.0),
            (15.0, 1.0),
        ];
        for (x, want) in tests {
            let got = h.cdf(x);
            assert!((got - want).abs() < 1e-12, "cdf({},) = {}, want {}", x, got, want);
        }
    }
}

  "is_equivalent": "no",

In the Rust version, WeightedHistogram::new(...) and weighted.hist.quantile(...) both return Result<T, E> and use the `?` operator: if an error occurs (e.g. invalid alpha or calling quantile on an empty histogram or an out-of-range p), the function returns Err and stops early. The Go Example() never errors or aborts, whereas the Rust example() can return Err under the same inputs.",
  "corner_case": "Calling quantile on an empty weighted histogram: in Go, w.Quantile(0.25) returns NaN (no error); in Rust, w.quantile(0.25)? returns Err and example() returns early with an error."
}
</final_response_format>

In the Rust version, WeightedHistogram::new(...) and weighted.hist.quantile(...) both return Result<T, E> and use the `?` operator: if an error occurs (e.g. invalid alpha or calling quantile on an empty histogram or an out-of-range p), the function returns Err and stops early. The Go Example() never errors or aborts, whereas the Rust example() can return Err under the same inputs.

exception_error.result:
<final_response_format>
{
  "is_equivalent": "no",
  "explanation": "The Go implementation calls constructors and methods that do not return errors, so there is no error handling. In Rust, the constructor for WeightedHistogram and the quantile method return Results and are propagated with the `?` operator. Since the Go version has no error propagation semantics and the Rust version does, their exception/error handling is not equivalent.",
  "example": "```rust
pub fn example() -> Result<()> {
    // Create a NumericHistogram (no error)
    let mut h = NumericHistogram::new_histogram(160);
    h.add(160.0);
    // quantile returns a plain f64, no error
    let q = h.quantile(0.25);

    // Create a WeightedHistogram which can error
    let mut w = WeightedHistogram::new(160, 1.0)
        .map_err(|e| anyhow!("Failed to create WeightedHistogram: {}", e))?;
    w.add(160.0);
    // Handle possible error from quantile explicitly
    let qw = match w.quantile(0.25) {
        Ok(val) => val,
        Err(e) => {
            eprintln!("Quantile error: {}", e);
            return Err(e.into());
        }
    };
    Ok(())
}
```"
}
</final_response_format>

parsed_final_response.explanation: The Go implementation calls constructors and methods that do not return errors, so there is no error handling. In Rust, the constructor for WeightedHistogram and the quantile method return Results and are propagated with the `?` operator. Since the Go version has no error propagation semantics and the Rust version does, their exception/error handling is not equivalent.

spec.result:
<final_response_format>
{
  "is_equivalent": "yes",
  "explanation": "Both implementations construct a fixed‐bin histogram and a weighted histogram, add a single datum of value 160, and invoke quantile(0.25), cdf(18), count, mean, and variance. The Go and Rust versions share identical preconditions (max bins > 0; weight/alpha ≥ 0; quantile p in [0,1]), identical postconditions (count = total weight = 1; mean = 160; variance = 0; quantile(0.25) = 160; cdf(18) = 0), and maintain the same invariants on binning and running aggregates. The minor differences in error‐return conventions in Rust (Result on constructor and quantile) do not alter the underlying behavioral contract for valid inputs.",
  "formal_spec": "Given a histogram H with capacity B>0 and optional decay α≥0, methods satisfy:\n  • H.add(x): incorporate datum x ∈ ℝ with weight 1 (or weight w for weighted).\n  • H.count() → total weight W ≥ 0.\n  • H.mean() → μ = (Σ wᵢ xᵢ)/W if W>0, else NaN.\n  • H.variance() → σ² = (Σ wᵢ (xᵢ–μ)²)/W if W>0, else NaN.\n  • H.cdf(x₀) → F(x₀) = (Σ wᵢ [xᵢ ≤ x₀])/W.\n  • H.quantile(p) → inf{x | F(x) ≥ p} for p ∈ [0,1].\nAll edge cases (empty histogram, p outside [0,1], α ≤0) trigger well-defined errors or NaNs consistently.",
  "counter_example": ""
}
</final_response_format>

parsed_final_response.explanation: Both implementations construct a fixed‐bin histogram and a weighted histogram, add a single datum of value 160, and invoke quantile(0.25), cdf(18), count, mean, and variance. The Go and Rust versions share identical preconditions (max bins > 0; weight/alpha ≥ 0; quantile p in [0,1]), identical postconditions (count = total weight = 1; mean = 160; variance = 0; quantile(0.25) = 160; cdf(18) = 0), and maintain the same invariants on binning and running aggregates. The minor differences in error‐return conventions in Rust (Result on constructor and quantile) do not alter the underlying behavioral contract for valid inputs.
parsed_final_response.formal_spec:
Given a histogram H with capacity B>0 and optional decay α≥0, methods satisfy:
  • H.add(x): incorporate datum x ∈ ℝ with weight 1 (or weight w for weighted).
  • H.count() → total weight W ≥ 0.
  • H.mean() → μ = (Σ wᵢ xᵢ)/W if W>0, else NaN.
  • H.variance() → σ² = (Σ wᵢ (xᵢ–μ)²)/W if W>0, else NaN.
  • H.cdf(x₀) → F(x₀) = (Σ wᵢ [xᵢ ≤ x₀])/W.
  • H.quantile(p) → inf{x | F(x) ≥ p} for p ∈ [0,1].
All edge cases (empty histogram, p outside [0,1], α ≤0) trigger well-defined errors or NaNs consistently.

  "correct_target_method_implementation": "impl __Synth0__add for WeightedHistogram {
    fn add(&mut self, n: f64) {
        // Trim and update total before adding
        let _ = self.trim();
        // Insert or update bin
        for i in 0..self.bins.len() {
            if self.bins[i].value == n {
                self.bins[i].count += 1.0;
                self.scale_down(i);
                // Recompute total and merge bins if needed
                let _ = self.trim();
                return;
            }
            if self.bins[i].value > n {
                let mut new_bins = self.bins[..i].to_vec();
                new_bins.push(Bin { value: n, count: 1.0 });
                new_bins.extend_from_slice(&self.bins[i..]);
                self.bins = new_bins;
                self.scale_down(i);
                // Recompute total and merge bins if needed
                let _ = self.trim();
                return;
            }
        }
        // Append new bin and update total
        self.bins.push(Bin { value: n, count: 1.0 });
        let _ = self.trim();
    }
}"
}
</final_response_format>
